column1: 120+ action items across every page type, funnel stage, and AI search signal. The only CRO checklist your B2B SaaS team needs.

column7: What To Do and Why It Matters
A clear headline improves message match and helps visitors quickly understand the product’s value proposition.
A supporting sentence clarifies the value proposition and reinforces relevance for the target audience.

column3: Single Primary CTA Above the Fold
column7: Put one clear action button above the fold. Use copy like 'Start Free Trial' or 'Book a Demo'. Avoid weak labels like 'Learn More'. A visible primary CTA reduces decision friction and helps high-intent visitors take action quickly.

column3: High-Quality Product Screenshot or UI Visual
column7: Show a real screenshot of your product UI near the top of the page. Product visuals improve clarity and help visitors understand how the product works.

Include a short product demo video, 60 to 90 seconds. Video transcripts are increasingly indexed by AI search tools. A well-structured demo script that explains features as plain language benefits improves AI content understanding.
Add a strip of real usage numbers or testimonials: for example '12,000 users, 4.8 stars on G2, 40 million in pipeline tracked' or simply testimonials with quotes, images and title of the person
Show 3 to 5 testimonials that each mention a specific, measurable result. Result-driven testimonials strengthen credibility and reinforce the product’s value.

column3: Secondary CTA Mid-Page (pairing primary and secondary)
column7: Visitors who scroll deeper are often evaluating the product. A mid-page CTA provides a convenient way to convert once their questions are answered.
Keep your nav bar fixed as users scroll and include your primary CTA on the right. This keeps the conversion path visible at all times.

column3: Benefits Over Features in All Copy
column7: Outcome-focused messaging helps visitors understand the real value of the product rather than just its technical capabilities.
Address the top three buyer hesitations in a short section: pricing, setup time, and switching cost.

column3: Final CTA Section with Value Restatement
column7: End the page with a CTA block that restates your value prop in one sentence. Restating the value proposition near the end of the page reinforces the product’s benefit and captures visitors who are ready to take action after reviewing the content.

Your primary CTA button must be a colour that appears nowhere else on the page. Do a squint test: blur your eyes and see if the button still stands out. If not, change it. Clear visual hierarchy also helps

Add one or two short lines under every CTA button such as 'No credit card required, setup in 5 minutes'. This copy directly removes the biggest friction points at the exact moment someone is deciding.

column3: G2 or Capterra Badges for the Current Year
column7: Show your most recent review platform badges near CTAs and in the header. Being visibly present on those platforms, and showing it on your site, feeds that signal.
Place your aggregate review score directly beside your primary CTA buttons. Having your rating on the page in structured, crawlable text ensures it gets picked up accurately.
Use a dismissible banner at the top of the page for product launches or promotions.

column3: Live Chat or AI Chatbot on Key Pages
column7: Add a chat widget to pricing, demo, and product pages. Beyond answering questions live, chatbot conversation logs are a rich source of real buyer language you can use to improve your on-page copy and CTA messaging.

Trigger a modal when a user is about to leave. Match the offer to the page they are exiting from. A well-targeted exit offer captures leads that would otherwise disappear. On blog pages, offer a relevant resource. On pricing pages, offer a direct line to sales.

Build a branded 404 page with a search bar, links to key pages, and your primary CTA. A 404 with no useful content is a dead end. A 404 with clear links back to your site recovers visitors who would otherwise leave.
Visually distinguish your recommended plan with a badge and a contrasting border. This guides buyers toward the plan that converts best and reduces decision paralysis.

column3: Annual and Monthly Toggle with Savings Shown
column7: Include a billing toggle and show the annual savings clearly, such as 'Save 480 per year'. Default to monthly to show the lower entry price first. Make the savings obvious enough that buyers toggle on their own.

Put a complete feature table below your plan cards with clear yes and no markings grouped by category. This is one of the most visited sections of any SaaS pricing page. Buyers use it to justify their decision internally.

column3: Customer Logos and Trust Badges Near the Top
column7: Place customer logos and/or review platform badges near the top of the pricing page. Buyers on this page have high intent. Trust signals here reduce last-minute hesitation.

column3: Money-Back Guarantee or Free Trial Terms
column7: Display your guarantee or trial terms in plain language. Write it as a sentence or a clear badge. A clear, readable guarantee reduces perceived risk at the exact moment a buyer is deciding whether to commit.

column3: Sales Rep Photo in Enterprise CTA Block
column7: Show a photo, name, and title of the sales rep in your enterprise section. This humanises a cold form and increases completion. Buyers are more likely to book a call when they can see who they will be speaking with.
Use a waterfall layout where features increase from left to right (fewer on the entry-level plan, more on higher tiers). Helps guide the visitor visually toward the right plan.
Add an ROI section to clearly demonstrate the long-term benefits of your SaaS. Helps justify the price and reduces hesitation before committing.

column3: Outcome Testimonials on the Pricing Page
column7: Testimonials that mention measurable results make customer success stories more credible and help buyers evaluate potential impact.

column3: Custom Price Calculator for Usage-Based Pricing
column7: For usage or seat-based pricing, let visitors calculate their own estimate. Personalised pricing estimates keep visitors engaged longer and give them a concrete number to take to their buying committee.

column3: Clear Plan Descriptor for Each Tier
column7: Under each plan name, add one sentence describing exactly who it is for. For example: 'Best for small sales teams getting started with outbound'. Clear plan descriptors help buyers self-select without reading every feature.

If you have real promotions with real deadlines, show or highlight them as banners or pop-ups. Write the end date out as a sentence. Fake countdown timers are widely recognised by experienced B2B buyers and will destroy trust.

Write every feature headline as the outcome it produces, not the name of the feature. Instead of 'Automated Workflows', write 'Your team closes deals faster because follow-ups run themselves'. Outcome-focused headlines are more persuasive than technical labels.

column3: Annotated Screenshots or Short GIFs per Feature
column7: Each feature section should have a labelled screenshot or a short looping GIF showing it working. Write descriptive alt text for each image. Visuals make features tangible and reduce the cognitive load of reading about abstract software capabilities.

Pull testimonials that specifically mention the feature shown on that page. On a pipeline analytics page, use a quote from someone praising that exact feature. Page-specific testimonials are more persuasive than generic praise because they validate the specific claim being made.

column3: ICP Pain Point Section Before the Solution
column7: Describe the exact problem your ICP faces before introducing your solution. Use the words your customers actually use in reviews and interviews. When buyers see their own language reflected on your page, they feel understood, which builds trust before you pitch anything. If not, simply highlighting BEFORE in your headline and first fold and communicating the after through features/benefits works well too.

Show a two-column block with the situation before and after using your product. Write each row as a plain sentence. Before/after formats are easy to scan and make the transformation concrete rather than abstract.

Under each feature headline, add one short sentence about a secondary benefit such as 'also connects to Salesforce, HubSpot, and Pipedrive in one click'. Each integration name you mention adds a keyword match for buyers searching for that specific connection.

column3: Sticky Side Navigation on Long Pages
column7: Add a fixed anchor navigation for long product pages so users can jump to sections. This improves engagement on long pages and helps visitors find what they care about without scrolling through everything.

column3: Compact Case Study Card per Major Feature
column7: Under each major feature, add a small card with a customer name, use case, and one key result. Pairing feature descriptions with real customer outcomes is more persuasive than either alone.

List your integrations by name with their logos. Write a short sentence for each major integration explaining what it does when connected to your product. Buyers searching for specific integrations need to find confirmation on your page quickly.

If your product has multiple tiers, show a feature matrix on product pages, not only on the pricing page. Buyers researching a specific feature often arrive on product pages, not pricing pages. Give them the tier information where they are already looking.

Write five or six FAQs specific to each feature page: setup time, data security, usage limits, and comparisons to doing it manually. FAQ sections address the questions buyers have but do not ask, reducing friction before they reach the CTA.

column3: Social Proof Number Near Product CTA
column7: Add a line near the CTA like '2,400 teams use this feature today'. Specific usage numbers build credibility at the conversion point. Generic praise does not have the same effect as a concrete number.

Keep paragraphs to three sentences maximum. Use H2 and H3 headings every 300 to 400 words. Use bullet points for any list of three or more items. Well-structured content is easier to read, increases time on page, and reduces bounce rate.

column3: Key Takeaways Box at the Top of Every Article
column7: Put a styled box near the top of every article that lists three to five things the reader will learn. This summary sets expectations and keeps readers engaged because they know what value is coming.

column3: Contextually Relevant Sticky CTA on Desktop
column7: Add a sticky sidebar or bottom bar CTA that matches the article topic. If the article covers sales automation, the CTA should link to your automation feature page, not your homepage. Topic-matched CTAs convert better than generic ones.

column3: Inline CTAs at Natural Article Breakpoints
column7: Place two or three inline CTA cards inside the article at natural section breaks. Use a small product image and a short headline with a button. Inline CTAs convert better than end-of-article CTAs because they appear when the reader is most engaged with a specific topic.

column3: Contextual Exit Intent Pop-Up on Blog Pages
column7: When a blog visitor moves to leave, show a modal that offers something related to what they just read. A checklist, offer or template matched to the article topic will convert far better than a generic newsletter signup. Relevance to the page topic is the key variable.

column3: Small or Text-Only Article Header Image
column7: Use a compact header or no hero image at all. Large hero images push your content below the fold and increase bounce rate. Getting to the substance faster keeps readers on the page.

column3: Author Box with Credentials Top and Bottom
column7: Add an author bio with a real photo, name, job title, and one-line credential at the top and bottom of every article. Attributed content builds trust with readers and aligns with Google's E-E-A-T quality guidelines.

column3: Pro Tips and Expert Highlight Boxes
column7: Pull key insights into styled callout boxes with a distinct background and an icon. These boxes break up long text, highlight the most important points, and give scanners a reason to stop and read.

Example: goodrx.com/wegovy/wegovy-for-weight-loss-cost-coverage
(This is about adding expert pick/recommendation callout boxes inside blog articles, like a highlighted "our expert recommends" section. Different from Pro Tips, which are more about tips/highlights.)
For articles over 1,500 words, add a clickable table of contents near the top. A TOC improves user experience on long articles by letting readers jump to the section they care about, reducing bounce.

Add relevant product screenshots inside the article body where they naturally fit. Include descriptive alt text for each one. In-article product visuals help readers connect the advice to your product without feeling like a hard sell.

Add a testimonial slider or carousel within your blog content, especially on high-intent articles like comparisons or product reviews. It reinforces trust at the exact moment a reader is evaluating options.
An interactive before/after slider inside blog content to visually show results or transformations.

For best-of and comparison articles, use a structured HTML table with tool name, key features, pricing, and a rating column. Tables are one of the most scannable content formats and help buyers compare options quickly.

column3: Affiliate-Style Layout for Software Review Content
column7: For review posts, structure each entry with a summary card, pros and cons list, who it is best for, pricing, and a rating. This format maps directly to how buyers make decisions and is the most effective structure for comparison content.

column3: Styled Conclusion Section with CTA Inside
column7: Format your conclusion as a visually distinct box with a summary and a next step CTA. A clearly structured conclusion with a specific recommendation converts better than a paragraph that trails off.

Move citations to a collapsible references section at the bottom. This keeps the article readable while showing that your content is backed by sources. Cited content builds more trust than unsourced claims.

Offer a downloadable resource halfway through the article in exchange for an email. Match it precisely to the topic. A well-placed content upgrade captures leads from readers who are actively engaged with the content.

column3: Reading Time Estimate Near the Headline
column7: Show an estimated reading time near the title. This sets expectations and reduces bounce from readers who are deciding whether to commit.

column3: Last Updated Date on Every Article
column7: Show a 'Last updated' date on every post. Fresh content ranks better in search and builds more trust with readers. An outdated date on an otherwise strong article can cause readers to question whether the advice still applies.

column3: Social Share Buttons with LinkedIn Priority
column7: Add social share buttons prioritising LinkedIn and Twitter. For B2B SaaS content, LinkedIn shares carry the most weight because your buyers are on that platform. Make sharing frictionless.

column3: Related Articles Section at the Bottom
column7: Add three manually chosen related posts at the bottom of each article. Link between awareness content, consideration content, and your pricing page. This keeps readers in your content ecosystem and moves them down the funnel.
Remove the main navigation, footer links, and any exit paths from your demo booking page. One page, one goal. This change alone can lift demo conversions by 20 to 40 percent.

column3: Outcome Testimonials Directly on the Demo Page
column7: Place two or three testimonials on the demo booking page that include a specific result like '4x ROI in year one'. Outcome-focused quotes are the most persuasive at the moment a buyer is deciding whether to commit their time.

column3: Customer Logos on the Demo Page
column7: Show a logo bar of recognisable customers on the demo page. Buyers need to see peer validation at the moment they are deciding whether to commit their time.
Keep your demo request form to five fields: name, work email, company, team size, and one qualifying question. Every extra field reduces completion by around 10 percent.

column3: Value Bullet Points Beside the Form
column7: Put two to five bullet points next to the form explaining what happens in the demo. Write each one as a plain sentence: 'See the pipeline automation feature live' or 'Get a custom ROI estimate for your team size'. These sentences are indexed and answer the question 'what do you get from an X demo'.

column3: Confirmation Page with What Happens Next
column7: After form submission, show a proper confirmation page, not just a toast notification. Include a timeline of what happens next and links to case studies or your product tour. This page sets expectations and keeps the buyer engaged.

Embed a calendar directly on the page using a tool like Calendly so prospects can book their slot without waiting for a follow-up email. Instant booking eliminates scheduling friction and dramatically shortens time to meeting.

column3: Live Review Widget or Recent G2 Quotes
column7: Embed a live review feed or paste two to three recent G2 quotes on the demo page. Recent, unedited reviews are more persuasive than polished testimonials because they feel earned.

column3: What to Expect Timeline Below the Form
column7: Add a three-step visual below the form: 'Submit, we respond within 4 hours, you get a personalised walkthrough'. Setting clear expectations reduces post-submission anxiety and no-shows.
Every testimonial must include a real photo, full name, job title, company name, and a specific metric or outcome. Structured testimonials are more credible and persuasive than anonymous quotes.

column3: Testimonials Mapped to Your Top 3 Buyer Objections
column7: Choose testimonials that directly address your top three conversion objections, not just your most enthusiastic quotes. Match social proof to the specific doubts your buyers have at each stage.

column3: Video Testimonials on Homepage and Pricing Page
column7: Record two or three short video testimonials of 60 to 90 seconds with real customers. A spoken testimonial that includes specific numbers is more persuasive than a written quote because it feels authentic and harder to fake.

Build a dedicated case studies page that buyers can filter by industry, company size, and use case. Filterability helps buyers find a story that matches their situation, which is far more persuasive than a generic success story.

column3: Compact Case Study Cards on Key Pages
column7: Place small case study cards with a customer name, result, and a 'Read story' link on your homepage, product pages, and pricing page. Each card should include at least one number. Distributed proof points convert better than a single case study page.
Embed a live G2 or Capterra widget on your homepage and pricing page. Live widgets pull recent reviews dynamically, which means the review content on your page stays fresh without manual updates.

If your product has been featured in industry publications, podcasts, or newsletters, add an 'As seen in' bar with their logos. Named media references are an authority signal for buyers evaluating your credibility.

Display specific numbers like '14,500 active users', '200 million in revenue tracked', or '4.2 million tasks completed'. Round numbers feel invented. Specific numbers feel factual and are more persuasive.
Replace anonymous or obscure quotes with quotes from companies your ICP will recognise. One quote from a well-known brand in your buyer's sector is worth far more than ten from unknowns.

Show G2, Capterra, Product Hunt, Gartner, Forrester, or industry award badges where you have earned them. These carry high weight in buying decisions, especially enterprise. Only display awards you have actually received.
Every page should have one dominant conversion action. Multiple competing CTAs can sometimes split attention and reduce overall conversion rate.

Replace passive CTA labels like 'Submit' or 'Click Here' with outcome-based copy like 'Get My Custom Demo' or 'Start Building for Free'. Outcome-oriented CTA copy consistently outperforms generic labels in A/B tests.
Pair every high-intent primary CTA like 'Book a Demo' with a lower-commitment secondary like 'Watch a 3 Minute Tour'. This captures visitors at different readiness levels.

Inside blog articles, use styled CTA cards with a product screenshot and a short headline rather than plain text links. Visual CTA cards stand out from the surrounding content and consistently outperform inline text links.

column3: Image-Backed Inline CTA Cards in Blog Content
column7: Inside blog articles, use styled CTA cards with a product screenshot and a short headline rather than plain text links.
Add a fixed bottom CTA bar on mobile that stays visible as users scroll. Mobile users expect a clear, immediate next step. A sticky bar ensures that step is never more than a thumb tap away.

column3: Message Match Between Ad and Landing Page
column7: The landing page headline must closely match the copy in the ad that sent the visitor there. Message mismatch is the single biggest cause of paid traffic bounce.

column3: Single Conversion Goal Per Landing Page
column7: Remove navigation menus and footer links from dedicated landing pages. One page, one CTA, one outcome. Removing nav alone has been shown to lift landing page conversion by 10 to 20 percent.

column3: CTA Visible Without Scrolling on All Devices
column7: Your form or CTA button must be fully visible without scrolling on both desktop and mobile. If a visitor has to scroll down to find the CTA, you are losing a measurable portion of your ad spend.

Structure landing page copy in three stages: name the pain your buyer is facing, describe the consequences of leaving it unsolved, then present your solution clearly. This structure maps directly to how B2B buyers evaluate solutions.

column3: Social Proof Matched to the Campaign Audience
column7: Match testimonials and case studies to the specific audience segment the ad targets. If the ad targets HR teams, show HR testimonials. Segment-matched social proof is significantly more persuasive than generic quotes.

column3: Trust Signals Above the Fold on Landing Pages
column7: Place your G2 rating, customer count, compliance badges, and a logo bar above the fold. Remove purchase anxiety before the visitor reads the CTA.

column3: Short Form with 3 to 5 Fields on Paid Pages
column7: Paid landing page forms must have three to five fields maximum. Every extra field is a direct tax on your ad spend. Use enrichment tools to gather additional data post-conversion rather than asking for it upfront and losing the lead entirely.
If you have a real, time-limited offer, write the end date out as plain text. Fake countdown timers are not credible to experienced B2B buyers and will hurt your conversion rate.

column3: Thank You Page with Pixel Fires and Secondary CTA
column7: Your thank you page must fire all conversion pixels for accurate attribution across Google, LinkedIn, and Meta. Include a secondary offer or recommended content to keep the new lead engaged.

column3: Heatmap and Session Recording on Every Landing Page
column7: Install Hotjar or Clarity on every landing page. Paid traffic behaves differently from organic traffic. Session recordings reveal exactly where visitors stop reading, what they click on, and where they abandon the page.

Read your G2 reviews and customer interview transcripts. Identify the exact phrases buyers use to describe their pain. Use those phrases verbatim in your headlines, feature descriptions, and testimonial selectors. Voice-of-customer language resonates because it mirrors how buyers think.

column3: Specificity Over Generalisation in All Copy
column7: Replace every vague benefit with a specific, measurable one. Not 'saves time' but 'saves sales reps 4.5 hours per week on manual data entry'. Specific claims are more persuasive and easier to verify.

column3: Problem-Aware vs Solution-Aware Messaging Per Page
column7: Top-of-funnel pages like blog posts should speak to people who know they have a problem but have not yet identified a solution. Bottom-of-funnel pages like pricing and demo should speak to people actively comparing options. Mismatched messaging to buyer stage is a common conversion killer.

Your headline is your highest-leverage copy asset. Run at least three to five headline variants per quarter on your homepage. Even a 10 percent improvement in headline engagement compounds across all your traffic.

Identify your top five sales objections from call recordings and pre-answer them in your page copy. For example: 'Too complex to set up? Most teams are live in under 15 minutes.' Pre-empting objections in copy reduces friction before buyers reach the CTA.

Every headline must be immediately understood by your ICP on first read. Avoid clever puns or abstract concepts. If the headline is unclear to a buyer scanning the page, the rest of the copy does not matter.
Write directly to the reader using 'you' and 'your team' rather than 'our customers' or 'users'. Second-person copy feels personal and relevant, which increases engagement and conversion.

column3: Pain-First Copy Structure on Product and Feature Pages
column7: Lead with the problem before you describe the solution. Buyers need to feel understood before they consider a pitch. Problem-first copy structure consistently outperforms solution-first in B2B contexts.

column3: Data Points and Numbers in Every Major Claim
column7: Back up every benefit claim with a number: '73 percent of customers see ROI within 90 days'. Numbers add credibility and are more persuasive than qualitative claims.

column3: Social Proof Name-Dropping in Body Copy
column7: Weave customer company names into your page copy naturally: 'Teams at Salesforce, HubSpot, and Monday.com use this to...'. Named customers in body copy build instant credibility with buyers who recognise those brands.

Systematically test three directions for CTA copy on each key page: benefit-focused such as 'Get My Free Trial', action-focused such as 'Start Building Now', and curiosity-focused such as 'See What Your Pipeline Could Look Like'. Document results and pick the winner each quarter.

column3: Navigation Labels That Describe, Not Market
column7: Audit your navigation labels and replace vague terms like 'Solutions' or 'Platform' with descriptive ones like 'For Sales Teams' or 'CRM Integration'. Clear nav labels help buyers find what they need faster and reduce bounce.

Structure page copy to match the F-pattern reading behaviour observed in web users: the most important content goes at the start of each line and paragraph. Bold key phrases. Keep everything left-aligned.

column3: Avoid Jargon That Your Buyers Do Not Use
column7: Audit your site for internal product terms and industry jargon that your buyers would not naturally use when searching. If a buyer would type 'tool to track sales emails' rather than 'engagement analytics platform', your copy should match their search language, not your internal naming.
Create authentic urgency through real scarcity, real deadlines, or the cost of delay described as a consequence. Write these out as plain sentences. Never use fake countdown timers.

column3: Keyword Intent Alignment on Every Key Page
column7: Map each high-traffic keyword to a buyer intent stage: awareness, consideration, or decision. Then confirm the page copy, CTA, and offer match that stage. Mismatched intent is the most common reason a high-traffic page has a low conversion rate.

column3: Title Tags and Meta Descriptions Written as Ad Copy
column7: Treat title tags and meta descriptions as ad copy. Include a benefit or a clear outcome in every meta description. A compelling meta description lifts click-through rate from search results, which drives more visitors to your conversion pages.

column3: Core Web Vitals Pass on All Conversion Pages
column7: Achieve passing scores on all three Core Web Vitals: LCP under 2.5 seconds, INP under 200 milliseconds, and CLS under 0.1. These scores affect both Google ranking and user experience. Pages that fail CWV lose both search visibility and conversions.

column3: Dedicated Comparison Landing Pages for VS Queries
column7: Create dedicated landing pages for queries like 'X vs Competitor' and 'Best tool for Y'. These pages convert at three to five times the rate of generic pages because the visitor is already in decision mode.

Use professional, clean preview images for every template. The preview image is the primary conversion signal on template pages. A low-quality preview makes the template look low-quality, regardless of its actual value.
Write four to six FAQs specific to each template: how to use it, what to customise, which tool it requires, and who it was designed for. FAQ sections address objections and reduce support requests.
Link to three or four related templates at the bottom of each page. This keeps buyers in your content ecosystem and increases the chance they find the right template for their needs.

column3: CTA Placed Above and Below the Template Preview
column7: Place your primary CTA above the fold and again after the template description. Having the CTA appear twice means visitors never have to scroll to find the download action.
Credit each template to a named team member or industry practitioner with their title and a brief credential. Named authorship builds trust and aligns with Google's E-E-A-T quality guidelines.

column2: High = Major lift in signups, demos, or trials.   Mid = Noticeable conversion improvement.   Low = Incremental, marginal gain.
High = Multi-day or developer resource needed.   Mid = Half-day to full day.   Low = Quick win done in under 1 hour.
Not Started   /   In Progress   /   In Review   /   Done
Start with High Impact and Low Effort items. These are your quick wins. Then move to High Impact and Mid Effort. Defer High Effort and Low Impact items.
Review and update this checklist in a monthly CRO meeting. Archive Done items into a separate tab each quarter.
